name: Two-Chair Technique
tagline: Give each side of an inner conflict its own voice and let them talk.
situations: A harsh inner critic vs. a hurt part of you
Unfinished business with someone you can't talk to
summary: The Two-Chair Technique externalizes an inner conflict by giving each side a chair. You sit in one chair to speak as one part of yourself (say, your inner critic), then physically switch to the other chair to answer back as the other part. The literal dialogue surfaces feelings and resolutions that stay hidden when it's all in your head.
howItWorks: Internal conflicts and self-criticism often run as a tangled, looping monologue that never resolves. By physically separating the two sides into different chairs and speaking each aloud, you make the conflict concrete and give each part a genuine voice and a chance to be heard. This emotion-focused method, validated in research by Leslie Greenberg, helps soften a harsh critic, integrate split-off feelings, and reach resolution that abstract thinking can't.

steps.instruction: Set up two chairs facing each other. Decide which part sits in each (e.g. the critic and the criticized).
Sit in the first chair and speak that part aloud, fully and honestly.
Physically move to the other chair and respond as the other part. Let it answer back.
steps.note: Actually switch seats — the movement matters.

steps.instruction: Keep switching as the dialogue unfolds, until something softens or shifts.

whenToUse: When a harsh inner critic battles a hurt part
Stuck in a repetitive internal argument
whenNotToUse: For trauma or abuse dialogues without a professional present
If it intensifies distress rather than resolving it
When you need stabilization rather than deep emotional work
tips: Speak aloud and actually change seats — embodiment is the active ingredient
Let each part be fully honest before switching
Privacy helps you commit without self-consciousness
If it gets overwhelming, pause and ground

evidence.summary: Two-chair work is central to Gestalt and emotion-focused therapy; controlled studies (notably by Greenberg) support chair techniques for resolving inner conflict and self-criticism.
citations: Perls, F. (1969). Gestalt Therapy Verbatim. Real People Press.
